title: Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal honoring — server-side audit
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate first-party ad-tech placement quality; focuses on sale/share opt-out honoring.
evidence: Hourly report 'gpc_honor_audit' writes per-session GPC signal + sale/share decision to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: §1798.135(b) and CCPA Regs §7025 require honoring Global Privacy Control signals as valid opt-out signals. The UC audits every web session.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.135 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.3: Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal honoring — server-side audit.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.37.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When an inbound web request carries Sec-GPC: 1 but a downstream ad-tech call fires within the same session, the UC fires naming the session_id, the ad-tech partner, and the endpoint.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Sessions without GPC and no opt-out cookie do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=web sourcetype=access_combined earliest=-24h
| rex field=_raw "Sec-GPC:\s*(?<gpc>\d)"
| where gpc=1
| join session_id [search index=web sourcetype=adtech_call earliest=-24h | stats count by session_id vendor endpoint]
| table session_id vendor endpoint count
description: Audits compliance with the Global Privacy Control signal. For any session that carried GPC=1, any subsequent third-party ad-tech call is a direct CCPA §7025 violation that must appear in the annual privacy report.
value: Automates the evidence collection California AG investigations have asked for in recent settlements (Sephora, DoorDash).
implementation: (1) Ensure `Sec-GPC` is preserved in access logs; (2) list regulated ad-tech partners in an allowlist; (3) run hourly; (4) pipe to privacy-metric KPI. Indexes required: `index=web`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=access_combined`, `sourcetype=adtech_call`.
visualization: Table of violating sessions, vendor bar chart, single-value 'sessions honoring GPC (24h)'.

references.title: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) — Full Text

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorized privacy team operations** — The privacy operations team performs routine data subject request processing, deletion verifications, and privacy impact assessments that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These are documented in the approved CCPA workflow procedures and should be excluded by the privacy-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled data retention processing** — Automated data lifecycle management jobs execute scheduled deletions and anonymisation runs as part of the approved data retention policy. These produce expected data processing signals that are pre-approved by the Chief Privacy Officer.

3. **Third-party service provider integrations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate data processing under written agreements (CCPA service provider contracts) generate cross-boundary data flows that may trigger detection rules during normal operations.

4. **Consumer-initiated account management** — Consumers exercising self-service account preferences, privacy settings changes, and opt-out selections through the official web portal create processing events that align with normal business operations under the CCPA framework.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `ccpa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved service accounts, scheduled job identifiers, and known service provider IP ranges. The correlation search applies a left join against this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception has not expired.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `web` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§1798.135`. The control objective is: "Evidence that CCPA/CPRA §1798.135 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.37.3: Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal honoring — server-side audit." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `web` under `access_combined`, `adtech_call`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ensure `Sec-GPC` is preserved in access logs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** list regulated ad-tech partners in an allowlist. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** run hourly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** pipe to privacy-metric KPI. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal honoring — server-side audit," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the CCPA/CPRA control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`web`, sourcetype=`access_combined`) with a subsearch on `session_id` across the last 24 hours. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `session_id` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.37.3: Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal honoring — server-side a]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.37.3,reg=CCPA/CPRA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=web sourcetype=access_combined earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `count`, `endpoint`, `endpoint]`, `session_id`, `vendor`. Run:

```spl
index=web sourcetype=access_combined earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table count, endpoint, endpoint], session_id, vendor
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.37.3: CCPA/CPRA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Violating Sessions (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Vendor Bar Chart (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Sessions Honoring Gpc (24H)' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**CCPA/CPRA-specific failure modes:**

• Consumer Request categories: CCPA distinguishes Right to Know, Right to Delete, Right to Opt-Out, Right to Correct, Right to Limit. Detections on 'consumer request' must specify category — the 45-day clock applies differently to each.

• Verifiable Consumer Requests: the consumer identity must be verified 'to a reasonable degree.' Detections measuring request-to-response latency must start the clock at verification complete, not at request receipt — this is a common source of false SLA failures.

• Sale vs share distinction: CPRA broadens 'sale' to include 'share' for cross-context behavioural advertising. Detections on consent-to-share must use the current law wording; a CCPA-only detection misses the CPRA expansion.

• Do Not Sell / Do Not Share signal (GPC): Global Privacy Control must be honoured. Detections must treat GPC-asserting browsers as opted-out irrespective of UI consent state — the technical signal is authoritative.
grandmaExplanation: We help you track consent and choices over time so your marketing and storage stay aligned with what people agreed to.
